entry.title: From the Boise Bench to Flavortown 🔥: Local chef competes on Guy’s Grocery Games
entry.summary: A Boise chef made his way to Flavortown, and won big. Susina Chef Alex Cardoza made his first appearance on Guy’s Grocery Games on Wednesday night, a Food Network show where chefs compete in grocery store-themed cooking contests. The episode, called Fan Write-In, featured two contests selected by fans. Cardoza co-owns Susina, a local restaurant on the Boise Bench, with his wife, Tara Morgan. Susina is a pre-fixe supper club with a European-American, seasonal menu. BoiseDev Eats wrote a restaurant spotlight on Susina in 2024. Cardoza was also a semifinalist for a James Beard Award this year in the Best Chef, Mountain Division category, as BoiseDev Eats reported. Cardoza said that the opportunity to appear on Guy’s Grocery Games first came about during the pandemic in 2020, but the timing wasn’t right. He reached out again a few years later, after Cardoza and Morgan opened Susina. “I sent them a message and said, ‘Hey, it’s been a couple of years, but I like the show. I’d like to be on it,’ and they called me back,” Cardoza said. Cardoza said the entire experience of being on Guy’s Grocery Games was “surreal.” “I didn’t get confirmed for the show until five days from filming. Then they flew me to California, we filmed the show in a day, and then they sent me home,” he said. “Then it was 18 months before they aired this episode.” The episode, which aired last night, featured two challenges. The first required the chefs to cook a signature dish using only processed and canned foods. The second required each chef to cook a “Winner, winner chicken dinner,” with a catch: after the chefs shopped for their dish, they were told they had to switch carts. “It was interesting, because I went in there with an idea, with a handful of recipes,” he said. “… Then nothing that I prepared for mattered in the end, because I didn’t even get to pick my own food, so that made it kind of fun. I was like, ‘Alright, well, this is what’s happening. I’m gonna roll with it.’” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Cardoza ended up winning the episode and taking home a $14,950 check. “I’m going home with almost $15,000 that I’m going to invest in the restaurant,” Cardoza said. To see Cardoza in action, the episode is re-airing on Food Network on Sunday, June 14, at 2 p.m. You can also watch it on the Food Network site here (you have to connect your TV provider account). It will also be available on Hulu, HBOMax, and Discovery+ at some point — search for Season 42, Episode 1 ‘Fan Write-In.’ Advertise

entry.title: Former city council member Kenny Pittman running for Eagle City Council
entry.summary: A familiar face is hoping to make a comeback to Eagle City Hall. Kenny Pittman, who served one term on the Eagle City Council from 2018 through 2021, is running for a second term on the dais. He chose not to run again after concluding his first term due to family obligations, but chose to make another run for the seat after four years because he hopes to cut spending and reduce density while the city grows. Pittman is running against six other opponents for one of the two seats left open after incumbent city council members Helen Russell and Melissa Gindlesperger decided not to run again. His opponents are Eagle Sewer Board of Directors member Steve Bender, former Eagle Mayor Nancy Merrill, Beth Haney, Robert Gillis, Danielle Davis and Tom Letz. “I’m running for council again to bring my experience back to the council for accountability and to balance growth,” he said. “That’s what we need in Eagle.” Pittman has lived in Eagle for decades and has worked as the marketing president for pes-wies, a locally owned payroll company, since 2001. A big motivation for Pittman to run for a seat in Eagle’s local government again is how much the city’s budget has boomed since he left office. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. He pointed to the city’s budget, which has now crossed the $60 million mark, and how much that has grown since he was in office only four years ago, working with budgets hovering in the $20 million range. He said the council needs to be more accountable for the growth in spending and should be pushing for tighter reviews on a line-by-line basis of what the city can afford, and what it can’t. “That to me is not fiscally responsible,” he said, about the spike in spending. “We have to have accountability for that. We have to show why this is happening in our community and that’s important and I’m hoping regardless of the outcome of the election we’ll look into this and see what the focus needs to be so that we can afford as a city to provide for our citizens.” Pittman said the last two years of his term he served under former mayor Jason Pierce and questioned many of the projects he proposed to do over concerns about budgeting. He specifically pointed to the city’s auction of a planned park site on the western side of Eagle Road as a move Pittman objected to, noting that if the city had stuck with this park, it could have been done already instead of trying to fund the larger sports park currently in the works off of Highway 16. The city made a deal to purchase the more than 80-acre site for $10.4 million at the end of 2022, with the bulk of the purchase being made through the reimbursement of impact fees to the developers of the planned community Valnova. “I served two years under the former mayor (Pierce) and a lot of the things that happened when (former mayor Stan) Ridgeway was defeated, and a lot of the things Pierce brought to the council I was against because of the financial situation,” he said. “Every time something got passed, I would say, ‘Why are we doing this? We have no money that’s going to be coming in that can support these programs the way you want to build them right now.’ I always questioned that.” Pittman is no stranger to the shooting range and would love one conveniently located in Eagle to use, but he’s not sure the city should spend public funds to make it happen. He said, “there is no way” the city could financially afford to build a shooting range and maintain it within the city’s budget. Pittman said he is also concerned about the proposed location of the city-owned range off of Willow Creek Road due to the proximity of nearly 40 homes. Currently, the range site in the Eagle foothills has an archery range, but the city hasn’t allocated any funds to expand it to firearms in its capital plan. The range design company also filed a suit against the City of Eagle, alleging more than $300,000 in unpaid bills last year. The legal dispute is ongoing. Pittman said he isn’t so hot on the proposed alternative for the city to pay $250,000 to sponsor the nonprofit, volunteer-run Crowfoot Range on Avimor’s property either. He supports the range and said he would likely become a member if it’s built, but he has concerns about how much the city would spend and the benefit for all of its taxpayers. Crowfoot Range leaders told the Eagle City Council that if the city were to become the range’s title sponsor, Eagle residents would get a discounted membership rate. “Let’s say the membership is $100 and they get $25 off, and that’s great that they’d be willing to do that, but what is that as opposed to a $250,000 donation?” he said. “I’d like to call it a donation. How does that benefit the citizens of Eagle who don’t shoot? $250,000 is tax money, it’s revenue brought in by the residents of Eagle, and then those people that don’t shoot, their tax dollars are going to something they don’t have an affinity for.” When asked about the city’s purchase of the Orville Jackson House, Pittman said he was one of the council members who supported the $2.05 million purchase in the summer of 2021 to preserve the city’s cultural and historic past. But, he is not pleased with any of the four options a consulting firm proposed to the city earlier this summer, and would need to see the city’s financial status as well as explore other possible alternatives before making a decision. “We want to keep it within the city,” he said. “That’s my true and honest opinion, but financially, how do we do that?” Pittman said he is also a supporter of a recent proposal to change the city’s comprehensive plan to reduce density city-wide floated earlier this summer that is currently out for public input. He said the change would be a “good step” toward stopping developers from pushing large subdivisions with homes packed in on smaller lots, but he said the city also needs to push for changes when negotiating development agreements with developers to slow the impact of growth.

entry.title: Voters approve Meridian public safety levy
entry.summary: Across Ada County, election results for levies and city council races came in late Tuesday night. The election had an approximate voter turnout rate of 28.7%, according to the Ada County Elections Office. Here’s a look at the unofficial results for Meridian’s elections as of 10:56 p.m., with 97.9% of all precincts reporting for races and ballot questions. The city’s Public Safety Levy was set to pass with 66.1% in favor; the levy needed 60% to pass. As BoiseDev reported, the $5 million levy will go toward funding firefighter salaries, increasing wages for police officers, and creating a prosecution unit for the city. For the city council races, all three incumbents ran unopposed. This means that Council President Luke Cavener, and Council Members Brian Whitlock and John Overton will all retain their seats for the next four years. As BoiseDev reported, Cavener has been on the council since he was elected in 2014, Overton has served on the council since being appointed in 2023, and Whitlock since he was appointed in 2024. Advertise

entry.title: In aftermath of ‘Everyone is Welcome,’ one W. Ada incumbent loses seat, one holds on
entry.summary: Voters in West Ada’s Zone 3 picked a new face with an inclusive voice, after the district received national attention this year for ordering a teacher to remove a poster featuring multiracial hands and the words, “Everyone Is Welcome Here.” Teacher Meghan Brown took a decisive victory over incumbent Angie Redford, earning more than 60% of the vote Tuesday. In Zone 1, Board chair Lori Frasure fought off challenger Dara Ezzell-Pebworth, a social worker. Frasure won with 54% of the vote. Brown told EdNews her victory shows that West Ada School District leadership made a mistake by ordering teacher Sarah Inama to remove the poster from her classroom. “Now they’re trying to be heard,” Brown said of West Ada voters. “And I hope everyone is paying attention.” She said her volunteers knocked on over 1,000 homes in the past two weeks. “It was a big volunteer effort,” Brown said. In a Zoom call with reporters at 7 p.m., Secretary of State Phil McGrane said statewide turnout was about 21%. Blaine County had the highest turnout, at 47%, heading into the final hour of voting . Results are unofficial and collected from county websites and VoteIdaho.gov. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. EdNews followed 15 notable races in seven school districts. Winners are shown in bold: Zone 1: Incumbent Lori Frasure (54.3%); social worker Dara Ezzell-Pebworth (45.6%). Zone 3: Incumbent Angie Redford (32.8%); teacher Meghan Brown (61.2%). The two challengers, Ezzell-Pebworth and Brown, support inclusion and oppose private school tax credits. The incumbents, Frasure and Redford, are running on strong academic results and fiscal responsibility. They both declined to attend a League of Women Voters forum in October. School board races are nonpartisan, but the races have taken on partisan tones as the incumbents embraced conservative endorsements and the two challengers received support from a liberal PAC. Political action committees on both sides of the aisle spent a total of $15,000 to support or oppose the candidates. The Idaho Majority Club, a PAC that supports limited government and a focus on families, spent $5,578 on mailers to support Frasure and $4,008 to support Redford, a total of $9,586. The PAC this year received a $85,000 contribution from Make Highways Great Again LLC, based in Cheyenne, Wyo. On the other side, Idaho Students First PAC spent a total of $5,453 on mailers to support Ezzell-Pebworth and Brown and oppose the two incumbents. The PAC last year received a $20,000 donation from the National Education Association Advocacy Fund, a Washington, D.C.-based super PAC associated with the nation’s largest teachers’ union. A third Zone 3 candidate, Anna Marie Young, appeared on the ballot, but dropped out of the race to support Brown.

entry.title: ‘Not a one-trick pony’: Idaho wine of the year shines for versatility, longevity
entry.summary: The Snake River Valley is known for a range of wines, and there’s one variety that has stood the test of time. Each year, the Idaho Wine Commission recognizes a “wine of the year,” or a varietal that is important to the Idaho wine scene. 2026 is recognized as the “Year of Riesling,” a varietal that has been in Idaho since replanting after Prohibition and is celebrated for its versatility. “Depending on where it’s from, it has different aromatic profiles and palate profiles, but one thing that’s typically in common for riesling is the level of acidity,” said Meredith Smith, winemaker for Ste. Chapelle and Sawtooth Wineries. “It will always have a nice acid profile integrated into those aromatic senses.” Riesling originated in Germany’s Rhine Valley in the 15th century and is known for its ability to withstand cold temperatures. After Prohibition, there weren’t any grapes planted in Idaho until the 1970s. Riesling was one of the first varietals planted during that time, and Idaho wineries have been making it ever since. “For Ste. Chapelle, being the first winery in Idaho, they planted riesling, that was their first. A lot of it was that Johannesburg Riesling; that’s where it started in 1975,” Smith said. “… What’s nice about it is that it has stood the test of time in this area, because from the 70s, those vines are still producing nice grapes and making nice fruit.” Although the Snake River Valley is quite a bit different from Germany’s climate, riesling still grows well here thanks to the shift in temperature from hot summer days to cool evenings and the high elevation at around 2,800 feet above sea level. Idaho’s terroir, or growing environment, creates a riesling that tends to produce more tropical, citrus, and fruit notes. “Riesling has a tendency to take on its terroir pretty well,” Smith said. “… In Idaho, we’re at about 2,800 ft to 3,000 ft in elevation, so we’re fairly high; that is an advantage for us for riesling. Now we do get warm too, so you may get a bit more lush, a little bit more pronounced fruit notes, leaning more towards pear, apple, kind of white floral.” Some may view riesling as a sweet wine, but Idaho riesling is versatile and produces a wide range of styles, from very dry to sweet. “Riesling does well in Idaho because of its versatility. I’ve worked with it for years now and I’ve never made it the same way twice. I’m always experimenting with it because it’s got unique flavors, it’s got really good acidity,” said Will Wetmore, Veer Wines Winemaker in an Idaho Wine Commission video. “You can try it sweet, you can try it dry. It’s not just a one-trick pony. You can do a lot with it.” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. The consistent trait of riesling is its high acidity combined with aromatics. “Depending on where it’s from, it has different aromatic profiles and palate profiles, but one thing that’s typically in common for riesling is the level of acidity,” Smith said. “It will always have a nice acid profile integrated into those aromatic senses.” Smith makes six different rieslings for Sawtooth and Ste. Chapelle Wineries, and the key differentiator is residual sugar. The Sawtooth Bone Dry Riesling has less than 0.01% residual sugar, and the range extends all the way to the Ste. Chapelle Special Harvest Huckleberry Riesling, which has about 5% residual sugar. “You can’t take a Chardonnay and put a whole bunch of different sugar levels in it and get six different wines that are good. You can’t do that with a lot of wines. That’s what’s so cool about riesling: it’s kind of this chameleon; it has all these different facets, but it’s good in every single one.” The versatility of riesling results in a wide range of wines that are suitable for different palates. Because of riesling’s high acidity, it pairs well with a wide range of foods, including spicy foods. “Anything that has high acid, you could always do anything that’s creamy with that,” Smith said. “With a dry riesling, it always goes well with anything that’s kind of hot or spicy, so you always can do your Thai food.” Riesling’s aromatics and crispness also pair well with many lighter foods. “Riesling also goes well with salads; they go well with a creamy dish, and they also do well with chicken, any type of lighter-style dishes,” Smit said. “… I would stick more with fruits, cheeses, lighter salad dishes, chicken, veal, fish, and then any spicy foods.”

entry.title: Lenty, Jacobs meet in Garden City mayoral candidate forum
entry.summary: Garden City will have a new mayor next year, but it’s up to voters to decide who. On Tuesday, two of Garden City’s three mayoral candidates met for a campaign forum hosted by the Garden City Chamber of Commerce at The River Club. They discussed their differing visions for the role of mayor, how the city should tackle affordable housing, and growth in the decades to come. Current Garden City City Council Member Bill Jacobs and CWI Trustee Molly Lenty are both running for the seat currently held by longtime Mayor John Evans, who is set to retire at the end of 2025. Teresa Roundy, a political newcomer, is also running for the seat. Lenty pitched herself as more than a day-to-day manager of the city, instead casting herself as a “visionary” who would push Garden City to establish itself and its direction during a moment of rapid growth. “Bill talked about managing the city,” Lenty said. “I think the mayor does so much more than that. We don’t just need someone to manage the city, we need someone to help create it.” Jacobs touted his experience as a city council member for the past four years, which he said gives him the ability to “take the reins” of the city on his first day with the knowledge of how the city already operates, while bringing on new energy to engage more with the community. “Yes, we need to have a vision and we do partner and we do have great relationships with other agencies, we work with them very closely, but in the end, your water has to work, your sewer has to work, if you call the police they have to show up quickly with someone who is well-trained, responsive and compassionate. I don’t think you can diminish the operational role of the city.” We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Idaho’s municipal election is on November 4. Garden City voters will elect a new mayor and two city council members. In response to a question about how the city should balance growth with existing businesses and growth already in an area, Lenty went on the offensive about the city’s transparency to the community. She started off answering the question, discussing the city’s need to embark on a strategic visioning process similar to the work she did at College of Western Idaho as the school grappled with the need for its facilities and staff to keep up with rapid growth. Lenty then criticized a series of zoning changes the city has been considering in recent months, including the creation of a medium-density zone, and the need for the city to be more transparent about the impacts of changes it makes to the code and to the comprehensive plan. Lenty noted Jacobs has also called for more transparency at the city, but pushed him on why he hasn’t taken more action. She also said Garden City has been difficult to obtain information from and she’s been required to file a public records request, which she said during the forum has gone unfilled for thirteen days. Idaho code requires local governments to fill requests in ten business days. “Why haven’t we been doing it for the last four years while you’ve been on council?” Lenty said. “Why are we just now saying we need to do it?” She finished her comments touting her time as a leader in business and politics and questioned whether Garden City needed “an operations leader, or a visionary?” earning a big applause from the otherwise quiet crowd. In his answer, Jacobs then defended the need of the mayor to keep the lights on and systems running normally in a city as small as Garden City and the differing roles of city council members and mayors, who typically do not vote on policy items unless there is a tie. He said after Lenty mentioned her unfilled public records request in a previous forum, he inquired about it with city staff and was told the city has no record of a public information request she filed. He said the city has a tight budget and has to choose its priorities and while he admits the city’s website could use updating, there are other projects the city council has opted to prioritize. “Do we spend $10,000 on the website or do we prioritize police or the greenbelt or infrastructure?” he said. “This council has decided to protect and preserve the things that impact quality of life. It’s a matter of making those choices and making sure first and foremost, are we preserving a level of service? The city is a service organization. We have to make sure everything works all the time.” Both candidates say they want to welcome growth, but there were differing perspectives on how the city is doing currently. Multiple times during the forum, Jacobs mentioned his work on the Garden City Urban Renewal Agency’s board and the plans in the works to create a new urban renewal for the south side of Chinden. He said this district would fund key infrastructure improvements to utilities that are currently making development in this area cost-prohibitive for developers and the need for a variety of housing types to ensure that residents of all income levels can live in Garden City. He pushed back on comments from Lenty, who said she’s hearing from developers that they’re sitting on properties due to how costly and difficult it is to get permits to develop in Garden City, saying that while the city has room for improvement, many other developers have praised the small city in comparison to its neighbors for ease of doing business.

entry.title: Derek James Smith running for Boise City Council
entry.summary: A candidate for Boise City Council is hoping to change the city’s course on addressing homelessness. Derek James Smith, a former salesman who mostly recently ran an ice cream catering business, filed to run against incumbent City Council President Colin Nash in District 2. If elected, he told BoiseDev his biggest priority would be to roll back the city’s investments in permanent supportive housing for people exiting homelessness unless they agreed to regular drug testing and substance abuse treatment at nonprofit shelter Boise Rescue Mission. The district covers the West Bench and part of West Boise. Josh Ellstrom is also running against Nash for the seat. In his interview with BoiseDev, Smith spoke candidly about his own experience as a hard drug user in the past, his past five years of sobriety and how stricter laws punishing him for drug use could have helped him reach stability earlier. He said this experience guides his push to focus the city’s efforts to address homelessness on drug addiction treatment, instead of support services and housing. “If I could say anything that might counter that argument (that people should not be forced into drug treatment to recieve housing services), it would be that if I had gotten arrested with drugs on me, let’s say a year before I got sober, two years before I got sober, that would have been a price I would have gradly paid for the extra time of sobriety,” Smith said. “For our culture we’ve reached a level of permissiveness and I just find it unacceptable.” Smith also supports deregulation of zoning and building codes to address the city’s housing shortage, less city spending on affordable housing land trust projects in favor of church’s providing services to low-income residents and continuing the city’s push for more mixed-use development. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Smith said he was inspired to run for Boise City Council after seeing a small group of people in the homeless community while at a local dog park. He said seeing people living on the streets or in their cars in Boise says to him the city is focusing too many resources on Housing First policies, a federally recognized housing strategy that provides people with places to live and wraparound services instead of having strict requirements for sobriety and income before they can recieve assistance. The City of Boise is currently aiming to build hundreds of new permanent supportive units with a combination of federal COVID-19 relief funds and other combinations of funding sources over the coming years. Smith said the city should shift its focus to requiring drug treatment, including regular drug testing for residents of city-run homes for individiuals and families exiting homelessness. Instead of spending city funds on developing affordable housing projects, he said the city should partner closely with private, Christian nonprofit Boise Rescue Mission and encourage individuals and churches to support expanding their mission to support drug treatment for those living in publicly funded homes. The Boise Rescue Mission is Boise’s largest emergency homeless shelter system and is completely privately funded, allowing the nonprofit to operate its programming with a Christian-faith focus. For example, Boise Rescue Mission’s application for its addiction recovery program asks applicants to share how many abortions they’ve had, if they’ve committed their life to Jesus Christ, what church they attend and, also acknowledge the nonprofit’s teaching that “only a heterosexual lifestyle is an acceptable lifestyle to God.” Smith said he met with Boise Rescue Mission CEO Bill Roscoe about his campaign and he would lean on experts like him and “I would do everything he told us to do because (Boise Rescue Mission provides) results.” “When I was going through rehab, I would randomly have to pee in a cup because it’s a vicious disease and it’s not that…, ‘hey, you tested positive so we’ll kick you and your family out’, it’s ‘hey, you tested postive and we’ll take you to the Boise Rescue Mission and, you know what, if you refuse treatment then you go to jail’. It is unacceptable to allow people to slowly kill themselves on the streets. We need to put requirements on the services provided for these people, because when they fall, we need to be there compassionately, but with a loving hand guiding them back to sobriety.” To Smith, a major obstacle to housing affordability in Boise is government regulations. He said the City of Boise should focus it’s efforts on a “drastric reduction” of the bureacracy around housing development in the city so more homes can be built cheaper and more easily. To accomplish this he said the city should rewrite its building and zoning code to be as simple as possible so developers and contractors no longer need to hire lawyers or other experts to determine what can be built where. Smith pointed to New Zealand and its recent law change requiring the government to communicate in “plain language” as an example. He said if the city eliminates enough of these regulatory hurdles, it will cancel out the money the city has spent on gap financing to develop affordable housing projects. “I think we need to dumb it down in order to make building more accessible and easier for construction companies in the valley,” he said. “We have simplified the building code in a way you won’t have to hire a team of lawyers to build here. That’s going to save you, how much money? That’s the amount of money I would have given you.” He said the City of Boise should also “empower the bureaucrats” to identify ways to cut red tape in all areas of city government. This is the same concept behind when the city streamlined its licensing process for childcare facilities in 2023, prior to the Idaho State Legislature ending city licensing for childcare facilities. Smith is also supportive of mixed-use housing across the city, specifically in busy intersections and at the Boise Outlet Mall. When asked about the city’s zoning code rewrite, which allowed more density city-wide and increased the emphasis on mixed-use development, Smith said he was not familiar enough on the code to comment on what changes he would make to it. Currently the City of Boise’s zoning code requires a minimum of four stories in height at busy intersections, known as activity centers. In at least one case so far, this has proved difficult to implement because CC&Rs governing development at most shopping centers prevent mixed-use tall buildings developed on their property.

entry.title: Garden City P&Z Commissioner Starr Shepard running for city council
entry.summary: A Garden City Planning & Zoning Commissioner is hoping to get promoted. Starr Shepard, a member of the small city’s P&Z commission for the past three and a half years, is running for one of the two seats up for grabs on the Garden City City Council in November. If elected, she hopes to find ways to bring in more grant funds to improve sidewalk infrastructure, implement incentives to encourage affordable housing development, make the city’s zoning laws user-friendly, and provide more representation for residents on the city’s more urban east end. “I’m running for a local office because I believe in the power of community and our local government shapes our daily lives, how we move and how we live, but it’s not just about policies and it’s about people and listening to their concerns and acting on their ideas so we can work together to create a city where everyone has the opportunity to be seen and heard,” Shepard said. She is one of six candidates competing for the seats, with the top two vote getters landing a spot on the dais. Shepard is running against Kelceymarie Warner, Dennis Mansfield, incumbent City Council Member Teresa Jorgensen, Wendy Carver-Herbert, and Gage Coprivnicar. Shephard is currently a workplace investigator with J.R. Simplot after stints running community health programs on youth suicide prevention, substance abuse, and mental healthcare for frontline workers during the COVID-19 pandemic with BPA Health. She also previously worked for twenty years assisting immigrant women who were victims of domestic violence and sexual assault, as well as creating training programs for law enforcement to recognize and investigate human trafficking. Shepard also worked in Guatemala on economic development programs for Mayan women after the long-running civil war in the country ended. She graduated from Virginia Commonwealth University with a degree in Psychology. We need your support. BoiseDev provides fact-based reporting to our community free. It''s only possible with support from readers. Make a donation of any size here Or become a member for great benefits. Shepard said her nearly four years on the city’s P&Z commission give her a depth of insight into how growth impacts neighborhoods and how the city handles growth currently. She said her biggest priority when looking at how the city is writing zoning code changes and developing land use policies is ensuring that codes are easy to interpret. Shepard pointed to the city’s recent zoning changes, which upzoned some areas of the city and downzoned others, as an example of how the city should be changing its code so the rules are less subjective and keep pace with growth in the area. For example, Garden City repealed its Live Work Create District in 2022, promoting mixed-use development in the East End, after a court challenge found aspects of the district’s requirements subjective. Shepard said Garden City needs more housing to address the region’s shortage of homes, but after recent changes, she doesn’t think the city’s comprehensive plans and zoning should be changed further unless there is extensive public input for a new comprehensive plan. She does support more mixed-use and transit-oriented development for the land-locked city as it continues to grow, along with balancing the needs of current residents. “I believe people should feel secure in their right to use their property and that means making zoning and land use decisions clear and consistent and avoiding rules that make it harder for small businesses and families to use their properties,” she said. Another focus for Shepard is ensuring that residents of the east end, which historically has been home to most of the city’s mobile home parks and is now rapidly redeveloping, have representation on the city council. Currently, all members of the Garden City Council reside in the city’s more suburban west end. She’d propose a monthly town hall for all residents to share concerns and thoughts with city officials to increase connection with the community. “I’d like to use my experience on P&Z to work with city officials, developers, residents, neighboring cities, partner agencies, and different organizations to ensure open communication because if we can coordinate with surrounding governments and groups to better guide growth so it’s a sustainable balance for the whole community,” she said. “My whole thing is to build consensus so neighbors have a place where they can speak and have their voices heard.” Adding more infrastructure for pedestrians and cyclists, especially in the east end, is a priority for Shepard. She noted the piecemeal nature of sidewalk construction in the rapidly developing area, which leaves blocks with inconsistent sidewalk coverage. Sidewalks, bike lanes, and road infrastructure are governed by the Ada County Highway District, but Shepard said she would like to learn how Garden City could press for more improvements in collaboration with the transportation agency. “We have a lot of pedestrians, especially around 34th street, and I’ve seen people down there navigating pushing baby carriers and navigating cars and bikes and there are so many people down there that we need to have sidewalks,” she said. “…Right now we get sidewalks when we get a development agreement, so sidewalks are piecemeal, but if we can work to maybe make impact fees go into a fund so that one year this whole street gets done, so pretty soon all the streets have sidewalks, I think that would bring a lot to our community and that would help businesses because people would feel more comfortable walking down here and it’s safer.” She would also like for the city to consider hiring staff to be grant writers, contingent on finding funding to pay for their positions, tasked with helping the city secure federal funds to help build projects in Garden City that are out of reach of the small city’s budget. She cited recent improvements ACHD is planning, like an enhanced pedestrian crossing on Chinden Boulevard, funded by a grant from the federal government as an example.

entry.title: Bruce, Rocco come out on top in Kuna City Council race
entry.summary: A mix of familiar and new faces will head up Kuna’s city council next year. The local election in Ada County had an approximate voter turnout rate of 28.7%, according to the Ada County Elections Office. Here’s a look at the unofficial results for Kuna’s elections as of 10:56 p.m., with 97.9% of all precincts reporting for races and ballot questions. Current Kuna City Council President and Idaho State Representative Chris Bruce will retain his seat, and candidate Michael Rocco will join the council. Bruce and Rocco received the two highest percentages of votes in the three-candidate race, with Rocco picking up approximately 38.7% of the vote and Bruce receiving 34.4%. Candidate Stephen Irish picked up 27.1% of the vote and will not join the council. A total of 4,109 ballots were cast in the race. Advertise

entry.title: Phillips tops incumbent Wagoner in Caldwell; Tilmant wins council seat
entry.summary: A newcomer will take to the City of Caldwell mayor’s office come 2026. Caldwell voters took to the polls on Tuesday, delivering a decisive victory to Eric Phillips in the city’s mayoral race, preliminary results show. Phillips received 57.1% of the votes, or 3,906 votes. Incumbent Jarom Wagoner received 30.3% of the vote, or 2,073 votes, and Brad Doty received 12.6%, or 861 votes. Had Phillips’s votes dipped below 50%, the race would have proceeded to a runoff election between him and Wagoner. The city also had three city council seats up for grabs. Preliminary results show that Scott Tilmant secured seat number one, capturing 37.7% of the vote, or 2,489 votes, beating out fellow newcomers Brandon Weast (29% of the vote, 1,913 votes), Carlos Hernandez (28.6% of the vote, 1,887 votes), and Jorge Arancivia (4.7% of the vote, 311 votes). The seat will be vacated by Doty, the mayoral candidate. In seat two, preliminary results show that incumbent Chuck Stadick will retain his seat. Stadick received 47.7% of the vote, or 3,101 votes to Randy Neary’s 31.5%, or 2,045 votes, and Dennis Staack’s 20.8%, or 1,348 votes. And in the third seat, incumbent Diana Register handily won against challenger Marisela Pesina. Register received 64.5% of the vote, or 4,285 votes, while Pesina received 35.5%, or 2,353 votes. The Canyon County Board of Commissioners will vote to formalize the vote counts during a canvass scheduled for Thursday at 11:30 a.m. at the Canyon County elections office. Advertise
